summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions about graduate school trauma, conference etiquette, career transitions from academia to industry, and dating challenges. She proposes starting a feminist women's magazine with practical advice. The responses blend humor with genuine insight, covering topics from mental health coping strategies to the limitations of online dating algorithms. O'Neil draws on her own experience transitioning from academia to quantitative finance to advise a PhD student about industry opportunities.
key_points: A meditation technique for processing past trauma: set aside dedicated time to think about painful events, then mentally visualize them shrinking in importance as your life expands around them
Math PhDs seeking industry careers face a paradox: companies once trained candidates from scratch, but now demand specialized skills even for entry-level data science positions
Online dating algorithms use 'very few, poorly chosen statistics' that disadvantage anyone who doesn't fit conventional desirability metrics
The proposal for a feminist magazine focuses on practical empowerment: birth control choices, finding reliable tradespeople, and rejecting patriarchal pressure around body modification
money_quotes: Give yourself some time each day to think about what happened. The important thing about setting aside time to think about it is that, the rest of the day, you don't have to.

That room is your existence, or your mind if you'd prefer it, and that box is pretty small compared to the size of the room. If that box consisted of stinky cheese, it would be stifling if the room were small, but since the room is enormous and growing larger all the time, it's barely noticeable.

Don't do what I did, which was just move to the only job I absolutely knew about existing, namely being a quant at D.E. Shaw, simply because I got recruitment emails about the job and knew people who had done it.
Personally I was turned down recently for a data science job because I didn't have experience with a specific algorithm, which I found bizarre.
That's the shitty thing about online dating. They ask for very few, poorly chosen statistics, and if you don't fit into what people think is desirable, you're totally fucked.
key_insight: Whether dealing with past trauma, career uncertainty, or dating frustration, the solution often involves acknowledging constraints honestly while actively expanding your perspective and options beyond the narrow metrics others use to judge you.
